name: chicken with vinegar
instructions:
Samin Nosrat's Poulet au Vinaigre, from Salt Fat Acid Heat (pp. 336-337). A superior version of chicken fricassee — the fricassee recipe used to live in this cookbook but was removed after too many disappointing attempts. This recipe replaces it in spirit and gets the register right.

INGREDIENTS (Nosrat's recipe, verbatim):
- 1 whole 4-pound chicken (broken down into 8 pieces — see notes on why whole > pre-butchered)
- salt (generous; pre-salt the night before if possible)
- freshly ground black pepper
- 1/2 cup all-purpose flour
- extra-virgin olive oil (enough to coat the pan for browning)
- 3 tbsp unsalted butter
- 2 medium yellow onions, thinly sliced
- 3/4 cup dry white wine
- 6 tbsp white wine vinegar
- 2 tbsp fresh tarragon leaves, finely chopped (divided)
- 1/2 cup heavy whipping cream OR crème fraîche

PROCEDURE:

1. PRE-SALT THE CHICKEN: cut the whole chicken into 8 pieces. Season generously with salt and pepper. Ideally do this the night before (refrigerate). At minimum, salt the chicken an hour before cooking and leave on the counter.

2. DREDGE: place the flour in a shallow bowl or pie plate; season with a generous pinch of salt. Dredge the chicken pieces, shake off the excess, and lay them in a single layer on a wire rack or parchment-lined baking sheet.

3. BROWN THE CHICKEN: heat a large skillet or Dutch oven over medium-high heat with just enough olive oil to coat the pan. Brown the chicken in TWO BATCHES (don't crowd), starting skin-side down, then turn and rotate for even browning — about 4 minutes per side. Move the browned pieces to a baking sheet. Discard the fat and wipe out the pan.

4. OVER-CARAMELIZE THE ONIONS — THIS IS THE SECRET (see notes): return the pan to medium heat. Melt the butter. Add the sliced onions, season with salt, stir. Cook 25 MINUTES stirring occasionally until deeply browned — almost burned. This isn't 'softened onions' territory; you're pushing them past the standard french-caramelization point.

5. DEGLAZE WITH WINE + VINEGAR: increase heat to high, add the wine and vinegar, scrape the pan with a wooden spoon to lift the fond. Add HALF the tarragon and stir.

6. BRAISE THE CHICKEN: return the chicken skin-side up. Lower to a simmer, set the lid AJAR (not fully sealed), and cook. Remove the breasts around 12 min (they cook faster); let the dark meat continue to about 35-40 min total until tender at the bone.

7. WATCH THE LIQUID BUDGET — ADD WATER IF NEEDED (see notes): Nosrat's recipe has a TIGHT liquid budget and it's easy to scorch the pan before the chicken is cooked through. If the pan is drying out and the dark meat still needs time, splash in a bit of water to keep the braise going. This is a real practical fix; don't let the pan burn out chasing her original ratios.

8. FINISH THE SAUCE: transfer the chicken to a platter. Increase heat, add the cream or crème fraîche, and let the sauce come to a simmer and thicken. Taste and adjust with salt, pepper, and MORE VINEGAR if the sauce needs a perk. Add the remaining tarragon and spoon over the chicken to serve. Serves 4-6.
notes:
SUPERIOR VERSION OF CHICKEN FRICASSEE: this recipe replaces a chicken fricassee recipe that used to live in the cookbook and was removed after too many disappointing attempts. Nosrat's take gets the register right where the standard fricassee formula never quite did.

=== THE SECRETS — WHY THIS WORKS WHERE FRICASSEE DOESN'T ===

OVER-CARAMELIZED ONIONS (ALMOST BURNED) — THE LOAD-BEARING TECHNIQUE: the 25-minute onion cook takes the onions past standard softened-golden territory and into deep, almost-burned caramelization. This is where the dish's structural umami comes from. Standard fricassee recipes stop earlier and rely on stock and mushrooms to fill the umami slot; here, the over-caramelized onions ARE the umami base. Don't rush this step and don't stop it early — the onions should read genuinely dark, edge-of-burnt.

VINEGAR — LOAD-BEARING ACID, NOT AN AFTERTHOUGHT: white wine vinegar is a full 6 tablespoons, not a splash. It cooks off the raw sharpness during the braise and integrates with the cream at the finish, but the acid remains structurally present in the sauce. The recipe title ("chicken with VINEGAR") is honest — the vinegar is a headline ingredient, not a background note.

NO MUSHROOMS, NO STOCK — 'UMAMI OVERLOAD' IS THE FRICASSEE PROBLEM: this is the specific insight that differentiates this recipe from chicken fricassee. Fricassee recipes typically load mushrooms and stock on top of the chicken-and-cream base, and the result reads muddy and umami-overloaded. Nosrat's recipe leaves them out entirely — the umami comes from over-caramelized onions and pan-browned chicken skin alone. The result reads clean and focused rather than muddy. If you're tempted to add mushrooms or stock, resist. That's fricassee territory (the failure mode).

CREAM/CRÈME FRAÎCHE FINISH IS SMALL BUT LOAD-BEARING: only 1/2 cup, added at the end, simmered briefly to thicken. It's not a cream-based sauce; it's a wine-and-vinegar sauce that's tempered/enriched by cream. The distinction matters — too much cream would tip this back toward the fricassee register.

=== TECHNIQUE NOTES ===

BREAK DOWN A WHOLE CHICKEN — DON'T USE PRE-BUTCHERED PARTS: like all good french chicken recipes, this rewards buying a whole 4-pound chicken and breaking it down yourself into 8 pieces. Bone-in, skin-on thighs and drumsticks and breasts bought pre-butchered will UNDERWHELM — you lose the carcass (Nosrat says save it for stock), the skin-on structural integrity is often compromised in pre-cut pieces, and you can't control the cut sizes for even cooking. The extra 10 minutes to butcher is what separates a great result from an okay one.

PRE-SALT — DO THE NIGHT BEFORE IF POSSIBLE: Nosrat's technique is to salt the cut chicken the night before and refrigerate. Salt has time to diffuse through the meat rather than sitting on the surface. Minimum: 1 hour before cooking, chicken on the counter (not in the fridge). Under-salted chicken is a common failure mode; over-salting is rare with pre-salting because the salt distributes evenly rather than concentrating.

LIQUID BUDGET IS TIGHT — ADD WATER IF NEEDED: Nosrat's recipe has a tight liquid budget and it's easy to scorch the pan before the chicken is cooked through, especially the dark meat. Her practiced liquid control must be pretty good; mine wasn't tight enough on the first attempt. If the pan is drying out and the dark meat needs more time, splash in a bit of water to keep the braise going. This is a real practical fix that Nosrat's recipe doesn't call out — she probably manages the heat lower or the lid tighter than I did, but for a first attempt, water is the safety valve.

LID AJAR IS DELIBERATE: setting the lid ajar (not fully sealed) lets some evaporation happen while the chicken braises. This concentrates the sauce during the cook. Sealing the lid fully would leave too much liquid and dilute the finished sauce; leaving the lid off would evaporate too fast and burn (the tight-liquid-budget problem).

PULL THE BREASTS EARLY: white meat is done at ~12 minutes; dark meat wants 35-40 minutes. Removing the breasts partway through and returning them to warm through at the end is what keeps the breast meat from drying out. Don't skip this two-stage removal.

TASTE FOR VINEGAR AT THE FINISH: the sauce may want more vinegar at the end than you'd expect — Nosrat explicitly calls for this. The braise cooks off some of the vinegar's brightness and the cream tempers what remains. If the finished sauce reads flat, more vinegar (not more salt, not more cream) is the correction step.

=== SERVING ===

SERVE WITH STEAMED WHITE RICE: Nosrat cooked hers in her college apartment with steamed white rice; the rice soaks up the sauce cleanly. Mashed potatoes or buttered egg noodles also work. Crusty bread for mopping is fine as a supplement but rice/potatoes are the primary starch pairing.

=== RELATION TO OTHER RECIPES IN THE COOKBOOK ===

- CHICKEN FRICASSEE (REMOVED): this recipe explicitly supersedes chicken fricassee, which was removed for repeated disappointments. If you find yourself wanting to make a fricassee, make this instead.
- FRENCH CHICKEN SOUP: shares white wine + tarragon + chicken thighs family. Different format (broth + dumplings vs shallow-braise-with-cream) but same french-chicken-and-tarragon axis.
- COQ AU VIN: also a french chicken braise, but with red wine (pinot noir) + mushrooms + lardons. Coq au vin uses tomato-adjacent umami paths that chicken with vinegar deliberately avoids (see the 'umami overload' note above).
